pattern: Any cyclic partition, once you cut the circle open at one of its own part boundaries, becomes an ordinary linear partition -- so trying every possible cut point and solving the classic 'split into at most k parts to maximize summed ranges' linear problem at each one is guaranteed to expose the true cyclic optimum.
hints: If you cut the circle open at a point that happens to be a genuine boundary between two parts of some partition, that partition turns into an ordinary left-to-right partition of the resulting linear array -- with the identical score.

So instead of reasoning about wraparound directly, try cutting the circle open at every possible position and, for each resulting linear array, solve the standard 'partition into at most k contiguous groups to maximize the sum of each group's max-minus-min' with a DP over (position, parts used so far).

The true cyclic answer is exactly the best score found across all of those cut points -- no cut can ever report a score higher than truly achievable, and the cut that lands on a real boundary of the optimal partition will find it.

edge_inputs.description: wraparound split beats the whole-array split
k forces a single whole-circle part
k larger than needed, same best split as with a smaller k
k allows only a single whole-circle part
k large enough that splitting into pairs beats one whole part
k equals the array length, allowing every element its own part
all elements identical, every partition scores zero
the best split wraps around the array boundary rather than sitting inside it
k is larger than needed; fewer parts than k is optimal
a big value at one end and a small value at the other, favoring a wraparound part

stress.description: random values with k scaled to a fraction of the array length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [nums, k]

complexity.time: O(n^3 * k) via trying every cut point
